data.title: Amazon's next round of job cuts starts next week - report
data.description:
• Amazon (AMZN) plans another round of corporate job cuts next week, following around 14K layoffs in October 2025, as part of a target to reduce around 30,000 corporate positions, Reuters reported, citing sources familiar with the matter.
  • The total cuts would be roughly the same as last year and could begin as soon as Tuesday, the people said. The layoffs could impact units like Amazon Web Services, retail, Prime Video, and human resources, known as People Experience and Technology, the report pointed out. The people cautioned that the details of Amazon's (AMZN) plans could change. 
  • The retailer tied October layoffs to the rise of AI software, and “this generation of AI is the most transformative technology we’ve seen since the Internet, and it’s enabling companies to innovate much faster than ever before.”

data.title: Japan’s January factory activity rebounds while services activity surges to 53.4
data.description:
The S&P Global Japan Manufacturing PMI increased to 51.5 in January 2026, surpassing December's final reading and market forecasts, preliminary estimates showed. 

Japan’s S&P Global Services PMI rose to 53.4 in January 2026 from 51.6 in the previous month, marking the highest reading since July, flash data showed. The latest result also signaled a tenth consecutive month of expansion in services activity, with new orders growing at the fastest pace in four months and overseas demand continuing to strengthen. 

Additionally, the S&P Global Composite PMI increased to 52.8, marking the highest level since August 2024, flash data showed.

Separately, Japan’s annual inflation rate decreased to 2.1% in December 2025, down from 2.9% in November and the lowest since March 2022. Meanwhile, food inflation dropped to 5.1%, while core inflation fell to 2.4%, still above the central bank's target.

Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi is preparing to dissolve parliament and call a snap election to consolidate power and pursue increased spending.

The Nikkei 225 Index rose 0.2% to around 53,790, while the broader Topix Index also gained 0.2% to 3,624 on Friday, extending gains from the previous session. The Japanese yen depreciated to around 158.7 per dollar on Friday, extending this week’s decline after the Bank of Japan kept its policy rate unchanged as widely expected, after raising it to a 30-year high of 0.75% last month.

More on Japan's economy:

data.title: Bank of Japan holds rates at 0.75% and signals "rate hike ready" as it upgrades growth and inflation forecasts
data.description:
The Bank of Japan kept its key short-term interest rate at 0.75% during its first policy meeting of 2026, maintaining the highest borrowing costs since September 1995, before February's snap election.

This decision was expected and supported by an 8–1 vote, with member Hajime Takata suggesting a rate hike while others noted balanced risks to the economy and prices outlook. 

The central bank stated it is ready to raise rates if economic and price forecasts are met, revising four of six inflation forecasts upward, with possible future rate hikes in 2025.

The board also raised its GDP growth forecast for FY 2025 to 0.9% from 0.7% and FY 2026 to 1.0% from 0.7%. The core consumer inflation outlook for FY 2026 was adjusted to 1.9% from 1.8%.

The Nikkei 225 Index rose 0.2% to around 53,790, while the broader Topix Index also gained 0.2% to 3,624 on Friday, extending gains from the previous session. The Japanese yen depreciated to around 158.7 per dollar on Friday, extending this week’s decline after the Bank of Japan kept its policy rate unchanged as widely expected, after raising it to a 30-year high of 0.75% last month.

More on Japan's economy:

data.title: Gold charges toward $5,000 as geopolitics and Fed concerns fuel record rally
data.description:
Gold prices climbed toward the $5,000-an-ounce mark on Friday, as rising geopolitical tensions and renewed concerns over the Federal Reserve’s independence fueled a record-setting rally.

Spot gold (XAUUSD:CUR) climbed to a record above $4,967 on Friday and was on track for a weekly gain of nearly 8%, supported by a weaker dollar (DXY).

Silver (XAGUSD:CUR) surged to a record high too, just shy of $100 an ounce, while platinum also set a new peak, as a key measure of the U.S. dollar headed for its worst week in seven months—lowering costs for precious metals buyers worldwide.

“Gold is undergoing a sustained re-rating as cracks appear in the post-World War II rules-based order,” Yuxuan Tang, head of macro strategy in Asia at J.P. Morgan Private Bank, told Bloomberg. “Investors increasingly view gold as a reliable protection against these hard-to-quantify regime-shift risks.”

Elsewhere, Poland’s central bank, which ranks first in the world in terms of reported purchases of gold, this week approved plans to buy another 150 tons as it braces for more geopolitical instability. 

The below chart highlights a sharp divergence in asset performance over the past year. Gold has dramatically outperformed, posting a price return of nearly 80%, underscoring its strength amid macro uncertainty and a weakening dollar. In contrast, the U.S. Dollar Index (DXY) has fallen about 9%, and the S&P 500 (SPY) delivered a more modest gain of around 14%, lagging well behind gold’s rally.

data.title: Friday's Economic Calendar
data.description:
9:45 AM PMI Composite Flash

The flash Composite Purchasing Managers' Index (PMI) provides an early estimate of current private sector output by combining information obtained from surveys of around 1,000 manufacturing and service sector companies.

The PMI manufacturing is expected to be nearly flat at 52.0 in January versus 51.8 in December and services at 52.8 versus 52.5.

10:00 AM Consumer Sentiment

The University of Michigan's Consumer Survey Center questions households each month on their assessment of current conditions and expectations of future conditions. 

No revision is expected for the final January from the preliminary at 54.0.

10:00 AM Leading Indicators

The index of leading economic indicators is a composite of 10 forward-looking components, including building permits, new factory orders, and unemployment claims.

data.title: Vimeo announces global layoffs after Bending Spoons acquisition
data.description:
• Vimeo (VMEO) has initiated global layoffs shortly after its acquisition by Italian tech firm Bending Spoons, marking the platform's second round of staff cuts in under six months.
  • The cutbacks will impact a “large portion” of the company’s total workforce. The former VP of Global Brand and Creative, Dave Brown, said that he, “along with a large portion of the company,” had been “impacted by layoffs.”
  • Bending Spoons completed the $1.38B all-cash acquisition of Vimeo on November 24, 2025, taking the company private at $7.85 per share.

data.title: T-Mobile US to redeem $3 billion of 4.750% senior notes due 2028
data.description:
T-Mobile US Inc. (TMUS) said on Thursday that its wholly owned subsidiary, T-Mobile USA, will redeem on Feb. 1, 2026, the full $1.5 billion outstanding aggregate principal amount of its 4.750% Senior Notes due 2028, as well as the full $1.5 billion outstanding aggregate principal amount of its 4.750% Senior Notes due 2028-1 held by Deutsche Telekom AG.

The notes will be redeemed at a price equal to 100% of the principal amount, plus accrued and unpaid interest up to, but not including, the redemption date.

Payment of the redemption price for the 2028 notes will be made through the facilities of The Depository Trust Company. Deutsche Bank Trust Company Americas is the trustee and paying agent for the notes.

data.title: Petco prices $600M senior secured notes to strengthen capital structure
data.description:
• Petco (WOOF) Thursday reported the pricing of its previously announced senior secured notes offering and has agreed to issue and sell $600,000,000 in aggregate principal amount of new senior secured notes due on February 1, 2031, which will bear interest at 8.250% each year.
  • Completion of the notes offering is expected to occur on February 2, 2026, and is subject to, among other things, customary closing conditions.
  • Petco (WOOF) intends to use the net proceeds from the sale of the notes, together with borrowings under a new term loan facility and cash on hand, to repay in full its existing term loan facility, to pay related fees and expenses, and for general corporate purposes.

data.title: Japan’s annual inflation tumbles to 2.1% in December; core inflation hits 14-month low
data.description:
Japan’s annual inflation rate decreased to 2.1% in December 2025, down from 2.9% in November and the lowest since March 2022. Meanwhile, food inflation dropped to 5.1%, while core inflation fell to 2.4%, still above the central bank's target.

Food prices in Japan increased by 5.1% year-on-year in December 2025, slowing from a 6.1% gain in the previous month and marking the slowest rise since November 2024. 

Core inflation slipped to 2.4% from 3.0%, the lowest since October 2024, though still above the central bank’s 2% target for the 45th straight month. Monthly, CPI fell 0.1%, reversing November’s 0.3% gain and marking the first drop in nine months.

Separately, Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi is preparing to dissolve parliament and call a snap election to consolidate power and pursue increased spending.

The Nikkei 225 Index rose 0.2% to around 53,790, while the broader Topix Index also gained 0.2% to 3,624 on Friday, extending gains from the previous session. The Japanese yen depreciated to around 158.7 per dollar on Friday, extending this week’s decline after the Bank of Japan kept its policy rate unchanged as widely expected, after raising it to a 30-year high of 0.75% last month.

More on Japan's economy:

data.title: Slm outlines 12%-14% origination growth for 2026 while launching new $500M share repurchase program
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: SLM Corporation (SLM) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Jonathan Witter reported, "I'm pleased to report on a successful year and discuss our strong outlook for 2026. Overall, the private student lending sector remains robust and is positioned for further success." He emphasized the impact of federal student lending reforms, stating these changes could contribute an estimated $5 billion in annual originations for Sallie Mae, representing approximately 70% originations growth over 2025 when fully phased in.
  • Witter highlighted a new private credit strategic partnership, describing it as "an innovative first-of-its-kind agreement...combines the more predictable earnings profile of our bank with the capital efficiency and risk transfer benefits of our loan sale program."
  • GAAP diluted EPS for the fourth quarter was $1.12, and for the full year $3.46, compared to $2.68 in 2024. Private education loan originations for the full year were $7.4 billion, a 6% increase over 2024.
  • CFO Peter Graham announced, "We continued our capital return strategy in the fourth quarter repurchasing 3.8 million shares for $106 million for a total of 12.8 million shares for $373 million over the full year 2025." He further stated, "Tonight, we are announcing a new 2-year $500 million authorization."
  • Graham noted, "Our net interest margin was 5.21% for the quarter, 29 basis points higher than the prior year period and 5.24% for the full year, up 5 basis points year-over-year."

Outlook

  • Witter stated, "We expect full year 2026 private education loan origination growth of 12% to 14%." Expected noninterest expenses for the full year 2026 are projected to be between $750 million and $780 million, with increased costs attributed to growth, one-time investments, and higher marketing and acquisition costs.
  • After 2026, the company expects to grow the bank portfolio gradually by roughly 1 to 2 percentage points per year until reaching a steady state annual growth rate in the mid-single digits. Approximately 30% to 40% of private student lending originations are estimated to flow through strategic partnerships.
  • Full year 2026 diluted earnings per common share is expected to be between $2.70 and $2.80.
  • SLM anticipates net charge-offs for the total loan portfolio to be between $345 million and $385 million for 2026.

Financial Results

  • GAAP diluted EPS in Q4 was $1.12 and $3.46 for the full year. Private education loan originations were $1.02 billion in Q4 and $7.4 billion for the year.
  • Net charge-offs for the private education loan portfolio were $98 million for Q4 and $346 million for the full year, representing 2.15% of average private education loans in repayment, down 4 basis points from 2024.
  • Net interest margin for Q4 was 5.21% and 5.24% for the year.
  • Delinquent private education loans (30 days or more) represented 4% of loans in repayment at year-end, unchanged from Q3.
  • Noninterest expenses for the full year were $659 million, a 2.6% increase year-over-year.
  • Liquidity at quarter-end was 18.6% of total assets, with total risk-based capital at 12.4% and common equity Tier 1 capital at 11.1%.

Q&A

  • Caroline Latta, BofA Securities: Asked about the impact of the postponement of wage garnishment and treasury offset on loan performance. Witter responded that the impact would be minimal due to differences in customer bases.
  • Latta followed up on modeling growth from the grad PLUS opportunity. Graham explained the incremental volume in 2026 will be modest and included in current guidance, with more significant increases expected over the next 2 to 3 years.
  • Giuliano Anderes-Bologna, Compass Point: Inquired about partnership and loan sale volumes. Graham cited a minimum $2 billion commitment for the strategic partnership and estimated about 30% of originations will be designated for sale, with seasonality affecting quarterly figures.
  • Terry Ma, Barclays: Questioned the return on increased noninterest expenses. Witter described the current investment as a "once-in-a-lifetime opportunity" and emphasized strong governance and measurement around ROI.
  • Ma also asked about credit assumptions. Graham noted that 75% of 2023 modification cohort borrowers are current at year-end, with this performance reflected in 2026 charge-off guidance.
  • Jon Arfstrom, RBC: Asked about EPS growth visibility into 2027. Witter said visibility hinges on the TAM opportunity and expense management discipline.
  • Melissa Wedel, JPMorgan: Raised questions about gain on sale margins and the share repurchase program. Graham commented on seasonal and transactional variability in gain on sale margins and outlined the disciplined approach to buybacks under the new authorization.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts raised questions around loan performance, expense growth, partnership volumes, and credit assumptions, with a neutral to slightly cautious tone, seeking clarity on the impacts of strategic changes and forward-looking investments.
  • Management remained confident and constructive, emphasizing strategic opportunities, expense discipline, and capital return, with Witter stating, "we are excited about our company's future and the opportunities ahead."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst tone remained focused on credit trends and strategic partnership implementation, while management displayed increased optimism about growth opportunities and strategic positioning.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance shifted from reaffirming 2025 outlook to projecting 12%-14% origination growth in 2026, with higher noninterest expenses expected due to investments and marketing.
  • Strategic focus evolved from announcing a partnership to operationalizing it, with about 30%-40% of originations set to flow through these partnerships.
  • Management tone grew more optimistic about the PLUS reform opportunity and long-term growth, while analysts continued to probe on credit quality and cost management.
  • Key metrics such as net charge-offs, delinquency, and originations were stable or improved, with detailed context provided for changes due to new partnership strategies.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management noted an increase in early-stage delinquencies but downplayed its predictive value for future charge-offs, citing stable late-stage delinquencies and improved collections.
  • The transition to selling newly originated loans and holding loans for sale each quarter is expected to shift reported metrics, but Graham emphasized this was "primarily driven by calculation mechanics rather than a change in the underlying performance."
  • Analysts highlighted risks regarding elevated operating expenses, credit performance in a challenging job market, and the successful scaling of new partnership models.

Final Takeaway

SLM Corporation concluded the year highlighting strong execution, strategic innovation with its private credit partnership, and confidence in capturing the PLUS reform opportunity. The company projects significant origination growth of 12%-14% in 2026 and is investing ahead of anticipated volume, while maintaining a disciplined approach to capital returns with a new $500 million share repurchase authorization. Management underscored the stability of credit performance and efficiency, while preparing for a structural shift toward partnerships and a more capital-efficient business model, positioning SLM for continued value creation in the coming years.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
